nativeRange.habitat: Large river systems with fast-flowing, well-oxygenated water; found over rocky or sandy substrates with large driftwood and submerged root systems; also occurs in floodplain lakes during high-water season

decorPreferences: large driftwood pieces (essential — primary grazing surface and refuge)
flat smooth rocks and slate for resting
caves and overhangs sized for an adult 50 cm fish
robust or artificial plants only (will uproot or destroy soft plants)
heavy external filtration — high bio-load species

diet.notes: Primarily an algae grazer and wood rasper in the wild; driftwood is not merely decorative but a dietary necessity — it provides cellulose, microorganisms, and biofilm. Feed algae wafers, spirulina tablets, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, sweet potato, peas), and sinking vegetable-based pellets. Also accepts occasional protein supplementation (bloodworms, sinking carnivore pellets) but a heavily protein-skewed diet causes digestive problems and should be avoided. Will graze tank glass, rocks, and substrate continuously at night. Does not reliably control problem algae blooms in a typical aquarium at adult size.

goodWith: large cichlids (Oscar, Severum, Green Terror) provided the pleco has adequate cave space
other large robust South American species
large robust catfish of similar size
avoidWith: other Pterygoplichthys or large plecos (territorial fighting; only one per tank unless 800L+)
small or slender fish that the pleco may accidentally injure or pin with its bulk
slow-moving flat-bodied fish (Discus, Altum Angelfish) — pleco may latch on and rasp slime coat
shrimp and small invertebrates (will be eaten or disturbed)
delicate plants (will be uprooted and destroyed)
nano or community tanks of any kind

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps; shows adult coloration and characteristic spotted pattern.
Wikimedia Commons image of adult P. gibbiceps; good lateral view showing large dorsal fin and reticulated spotted pattern.

summary: The Sailfin Pleco is a large Amazon armored catfish reaching 50 cm, widely sold as a juvenile algae cleaner but rapidly outgrowing most home aquaria. Requires 500L+ tanks with heavy driftwood. Invasive in Florida, Texas, and California. Specialist-only.
careNotes:
Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps is one of the most commonly sold plecos in the aquarium trade, frequently marketed as an “algae eater” alongside the Common Pleco (P. pardalis) at juvenile sizes of 5–8 cm for just a few dollars. This marketing severely understates the long-term commitment: adults routinely reach 45–50 cm total length, with exceptional specimens exceeding 50 cm, and they live 15–20 years in appropriate conditions. A minimum tank of 500 L (approximately 130 US gal) with at least 180 cm footprint is required for a single adult; most hobbyist tanks of 200–300 L that are suitable for purchase-time juveniles become wholly inadequate within 1–3 years.

Driftwood is a dietary and behavioral requirement, not optional decoration. Provide multiple large pieces for grazing and refuge. The species produces an exceptionally high bio-load relative to its size; oversized, high-flow mechanical and biological filtration is essential. The Sailfin Pleco is primarily nocturnal and will be largely inactive during the day. Juveniles may share a tank but adults become strongly territorial and conspecific aggression escalates with maturity — only one adult per tank unless the aquarium exceeds 800 L.

A critical welfare concern is the widespread practice of releasing specimens that have outgrown their tanks into waterways. P. gibbiceps is now established as a harmful invasive species in Florida (including the Peace, Hillsborough, and Alafia rivers), Texas, California (Sacramento–San Joaquin delta), Mexico, and Vietnam. It outcompetes native benthic fish, disrupts substrate, and is extremely cold-sensitive but survives in warm-water year-round states. Possession is regulated in some US jurisdictions; prospective keepers should verify local laws before purchase. Rehoming to public aquaria or specialist keepers — never outdoor release — is the responsible exit option.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria due to size requirements. In the wild, P. gibbiceps breeds during the wet season; pairs excavate burrows in steep riverbanks up to 1.5 m deep. The male guards and fans the clutch of 50–300 yellow to orange eggs. Captive breeding has been achieved in very large specialist setups (1000L+) with a soft clay or sand bank and seasonal water-parameter cycling (temperature drop, lowered pH, increased water flow) to simulate the onset of the rainy season. Fry are large at hatching and initially feed on yolk sac before transitioning to algae wafers and biofilm.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 50 cm TL), temperature range 23–30°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 2–20 dH, native range (Amazon and Orinoco), biotope, diet (algae, wood, organic matter), territorial behavior, driftwood requirement, tank size recommendations, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 50 cm TL), native range (Amazon basin: Brazil, Peru, Bolivia, Colombia), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), temperature tolerance, diet classification (herbivore/algae grazer and detritivore), and lifespan data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade perspective on pleco sizes, common hobbyist mistakes (underestimating adult size), driftwood diet requirement, feeding recommendations (algae wafers, vegetables), and tank size considerations for large plecos.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps profile
additional.notes: Species profile covering adult size, care requirements, invasive species status in Florida and Texas, trade practices, and specialist hobbyist recommendations. Confirmed invasive range data and conspecific aggression at adult sizes.

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species — Pterygoplichthys gibbiceps
additional.notes: USGS NAS database; documented introduced/invasive population records for Florida, Texas, and California. Confirmed the Sacramento delta (California) introduction. Used to validate hobbyistAdvisory invasive range statements.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; requires 1000L+ specialist setup with deep clay/sand bank for burrow excavation, seasonal wet/dry cycling, and significant space. Male guards eggs; fry large at hatching. Commercially bred in Asia and Florida fish farms for the trade.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 50cm+; needs 500L+ tanks. Invasive in many US states (FL/TX/CA Sacramento delta). Possession may be regulated. Sold tiny as algae cleaner but rapidly outgrows.
